content: You are a helpful assistant.

I need your assistance in reproducing bug reports for Android UI testing on the emulator. Our goal is not just to follow the steps leading to where the bug occurs in the app, but also to verify that the buggy behavior specified in the bug report is indeed triggered. During the initial prompt, I will give you the app name, bug report, and details about the app screen. Along with each prompt, I will also provide a screenshot of the current device screen. Use the screenshot to understand the visual state of the app — this helps you identify colors, visual elements, hidden controls (like video player overlays), notification content, permission dialogs, and elements that may not appear in the UI hierarchy text. The screenshot is your UIAutomator Viewer — use it to SEE the screen just as a human tester would. Your role is to provide suggestion at a time. After executing your suggestion, I will update you the details about the current app screen. We will proceed the iterative cycle of prompting the details of the current screen, receiving a response (suggestion) from you, executing the suggested action, and continuing the process until we successfully trigger the bug described in the bug report.
Please provide the app name, bug report, and details about the app screen to begin the process.

The details about the current app page include the activity name and the available UI widgets on the current page. UI infomration is presenting in group. Each group may hold a single widget or multiple widgets, such as [w1] and [w1, w2]. When a group holds multiple widgets, they usually perform related functions and can offer better insights into the purpose of that group. If the group has a identifier(e.g resource-id), the representation will be group_id:[w1, w2,...]. In addition, I'll provide the type of each group for a more comprehensive understanding of the group. Every widget is represented by its feature, which could be its text, content description, resource ID, or bounds. In our interation, we uniformly referred to representative feature of any widget, which might be its text, content description, resource ID, or boundary, as its 'feature'. Widgets also might be presented as dictionaries that include their class name as a key, such as {className: feature}, such as {NAF: featuer}. Note that className is not the feature of the widget, for example, given a widget {'NAF': '[0,1][2,2]'}, This is a NAF widget and its featture is [0,1][2,2], it representative feature is bounds becasue its text, content description, resource ID are all empty.
Thank you for the information. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

Your suggestion will be presented as a list, Suggestion:[a1], [a1, a2], etc. Plase note that you should either suggest available widget(s) from the given ui information or suggest action that no associate with a sprcify widget. In this list, each suggested action should be a dictionary with keys such as 'action' indicating the intended action, 'feature' specifying the  feature of the target widget, if the widget has multiple feature, just pick one, 'input_text' for any text that needs to be inputted, 'from_direction' is where the direction starts from, 'to_direction' is where the direction to, for any specific direction the action requires, 'target_status' for the desired status the widget should achieve,  'current_status' to represent the current status of a widget before an action is performed, 'index' for widget with the same repreentative feature, the idex of the first widget is 0. where each key should be included only if it's relevant to the suggested action. Actions that are available in this task, 1. 'click'. 2.'long_click'. 3.'set_text', if there are no explicit input_tex, feel free to generate an resonable input based on the current UI. Otherwise, try the given input_tex first. If the given input_tex is not working, feel free to generate any reasonable text in a different pattern than the given input_tex such as different length, cases, digit combination, etc. 4. 'restart' (to restart the app). 4.'orientation' (to change the device's orientation directions): 'left', 'right', 'upsidedown', 'natural', and 'left' and 'right' are landscape, while 'upsidedown' and 'natural' are portrait. 5.'swipe': This action has the following directions: 'up': swipe up is swipe from bottom side to  top side, 'down':'swipe down is swipe from top sideto bottom side', 'left': swipe left is swipe from right side to left side , and 'right': swipe right is swipe from the left side to the right side.  6.'back' (to press the back button). 7.'scroll': This action has the following target direction: 'up': you're moving the view of your screen upward to see the information, text, or images that are higher on the page, or to go back to something you've already seen. 'down':  moving the view of your screen down to see further information, text, or images that are lower on the page. 'End': fling to end vertically. 8.'home' (to press the home button and return to the device home screen — use when the bug requires navigating away from the app or returning to the launcher). 9.'open_notifications' (to pull down the notification shade from the top of the screen — use this when the bug involves notifications, status bar items, or quick settings). 10.'tap_screen': This action requires 'x' and 'y' coordinate parameters to tap a specific point on the screen. Use this when you can see an element in the annotated screenshot (by its numbered bounding box) that is NOT listed in the UI hierarchy text, such as custom drawn views, video player controls, overlay buttons, or canvas elements. Reference the element index numbers and bounds in the annotated screenshot to determine the correct coordinates. 11.'swipe_region': This action requires 'start_x', 'start_y', 'end_x', 'end_y' coordinate parameters to swipe between two specific screen points. Use this for precise gestures like dismissing specific notification items, drag-and-drop operations, or interacting with custom gesture areas visible in the annotated screenshot. There are two results after reproducing steps mentioned in the bug report. 1. Success:the buggy behavior specified in the bug report is triggered, for instance, if the bug results in an app crash, you have to ensure the crash message is on the screen. If sucess, please return a dictionary with the following keys: 'result' to denote 'success', 'reason' to explain your rationale, and 'bugreport' to document a more clear and readable bug report based on the actions we've executed in this process to. such as Suggestion:[{'result': 'success', reason': 'x'}] 2. Fail: If the buggy behavior is not triggered, you have the option to either continue with the iterative cycle or conclude with a 'fail' outcome. This decision should be based on whether you believe all necessary steps have been thoroughly performed or do you think is it possible to perform all(if you try too many steps, it's ok to give up and just terminate the process as fail), as reflected in the given information
Understood. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

When the target widget is missing, following these rules: 

1. Try scroll down to see if the target wideget is hiding down there when you think the current page is the target page. 

2. Treat the current page as the first level and prioritize the available widgets that are most likely to lead you to the target widgets, and systematically explore  every available widgets on this level to find missing target widgets. 
3. The widgets that appear after performing an action on a widget from the current level are considered as children and belong to the second level.
4. Do not start exploring the second level until you have tried all the widgets on the first level.
5. If the target widget is not found on the current level, use the 'back' action to return to the previous level and try another widget from that level. For example, "Suggestion: [{'action':'back'}]".
6. Continue exploring each level, moving from one level to the next only after all widgets on the current level have been examined.
7. NAF widgest might be the key
Understood. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — Visual Rendering Bug Detection: Along with each step, I will provide a [Color Analysis] section that reports the dominant pixel colors sampled from key screen regions (Status Bar, Toolbar/Header, Content Area, Navigation Bar). Each color is given as a human-readable name, hex code, and percentage. I may also provide a [Color Change Detected] section that shows what changed since the previous step. Additionally, when image elements are detected on screen (notification artwork, album art, thumbnails, large images), I will provide an [Element Color Sampling] section that reports the pixel colors of those specific image regions. This is critical for bugs where the wrong image is displayed (e.g., wrong album art in notification after skipping a song) — the UI hierarchy only says 'ImageView exists' but cannot tell what image is rendered. The color fingerprint lets you compare if the image actually changed. Use this data to detect visual rendering bugs such as wrong colors, theme glitches, wrong images, or unexpected visual changes. CRITICAL: For color-related and image-related bugs, the hex values in [Color Analysis] and [Element Color Sampling] are the GROUND TRUTH — they are sampled directly from raw screenshot pixels and are MORE accurate than your visual interpretation of the screenshot image. If the hex values show no change between steps, then the visual did NOT change. Do NOT override this data with your own visual judgment. Do NOT declare success for a visual bug unless the hex values confirm the change.

Understood. I will use the Color Analysis data to detect visual rendering bugs by comparing concrete hex color values across steps. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — Logcat Exception Monitoring: After each action step, the system monitors Android's error-level logcat for exception keywords that indicate silent failures. If exceptions are detected, you will see a [Logcat Exception Monitor] section in the execution status reporting the exception type and log line. This is critical for bugs where an operation APPEARS to succeed on screen (e.g., backup completes, file saves, export finishes, photo captures) but actually produces corrupted, empty, or missing output. Common exceptions: IOException (file write failed), ZipException (corrupt archive), FileNotFoundException, NullPointerException, SecurityException (permission denied), OutOfMemoryError, CameraAccessException. When you see a [Logcat Exception Monitor] alert, treat it as STRONG EVIDENCE that the operation failed silently. If the detected exception matches the expected failure described in the bug report (e.g., bug says 'creates 0-byte ZIP' and logcat shows IOException or ZipException during export), you should declare the bug as successfully reproduced. Do NOT ignore logcat exceptions just because the UI looks normal — silent failures are invisible on screen by definition.

Understood. I will treat Logcat Exception Monitor alerts as strong evidence of silent failures. If the detected exception matches the bug report's expected failure mode, I will declare the bug as successfully reproduced even if the screen shows no visible error. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — Multi-Touch and Complex Gesture Actions: The following actions use real simultaneous multi-finger touch events via ADB and are required for bugs involving pinch zoom, two-finger gestures, drag-and-drop, or rotation. You MUST use these instead of regular 'swipe' or 'pinch' when the bug report involves multi-touch. 12.'pinch': Real two-finger pinch zoom. 'pinch_type' is 'in' (zoom out, fingers move together) or 'out' (zoom in, fingers move apart). Optional 'x' and 'y' for center point. Example: {'action':'pinch','pinch_type':'in'} or {'action':'pinch','pinch_type':'out','x':540,'y':960}. CRITICAL: Use this for any bug involving pinch-to-zoom, canvas zoom, map zoom, image zoom. This sends REAL simultaneous two-finger events that the app's gesture detector recognises as a pinch. 13.'two_finger_swipe': Two simultaneous fingers swiping in a direction. Requires 'to_direction' (up/down/left/right). Optional 'x','y' for center. Example: {'action':'two_finger_swipe','to_direction':'up'}. Use for two-finger scroll, two-finger pan in canvas/map apps, pull-to-refresh with two fingers. 14.'two_finger_tap': Two fingers tapping simultaneously at a location. Optional 'x','y'. Example: {'action':'two_finger_tap','x':540,'y':960}. Use for zoom-to-fit, reset zoom, or any two-finger tap shortcut. 15.'three_finger_swipe': Three simultaneous fingers swiping. Requires 'to_direction'. Example: {'action':'three_finger_swipe','to_direction':'up'}. Use for screenshot gestures, app drawer, or three-finger shortcuts. 16.'drag_and_drop': Long-press then drag from one point to another. Requires 'start_x','start_y','end_x','end_y'. Optional 'hold_ms' (default 800ms). Example: {'action':'drag_and_drop','start_x':200,'start_y':500,'end_x':200,'end_y':200}. Use for reordering list items, moving widgets, drag-to-delete. CRITICAL DRAG HANDLE RULE: When reordering list items, ALWAYS look for a dedicated drag handle widget in the UI hierarchy or annotated screenshot. Drag handles are typically identified by: (a) description containing 'Move', 'Drag', 'Reorder', or 'Handle', (b) resource ID containing 'drag_handle', 'reorder', or 'handle', (c) an icon (often three horizontal lines or dots) on the far right or left side of a list item. You MUST start the drag from the CENTER of the drag handle, NOT from the item body. Dragging from the item body will trigger multi-select mode instead of reorder mode. For drag handles, use a shorter hold_ms of 200. Example with drag handle at bounds [937,354][1069,486]: {'action':'drag_and_drop','start_x':1003,'start_y':420,'end_x':1003,'end_y':574,'hold_ms':200}. If no drag handle is visible, then use long_click on the item first to enter reorder mode, then use drag_and_drop. 17.'rotate_gesture': Two-finger rotation around a center point. Optional 'x','y' for center, 'degrees' (default 90), 'clockwise' (true/false). Example: {'action':'rotate_gesture','degrees':90,'clockwise':true}. Use for rotating images in photo editors or maps. IMPORTANT: For bugs involving pinch zoom (e.g. 'zoom in', 'zoom out', 'pinch to zoom', 'two finger zoom'), you MUST use the 'pinch' action with pinch_type 'out' to zoom in and pinch_type 'in' to zoom out. Do NOT use regular 'swipe' for zoom operations.

Understood. I will use the multi-touch gesture actions for bugs involving pinch zoom, two-finger gestures, drag-and-drop, and rotation. For pinch zoom: 'pinch' with pinch_type 'out' zooms in (fingers apart) and pinch_type 'in' zooms out (fingers together). These send real simultaneous multi-finger events. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — Timing-Sensitive Tap Action: 18.'quick_tap': Taps a widget with minimal delay, bypassing the normal inter-step wait. Use this ONLY when the bug requires tapping a button immediately after a UI transition — for example, tapping a button before a dismiss animation completes, tapping a dialog button immediately after the dialog appears, or tapping a control before a short timer expires. Required key: 'feature' (text/content-desc/resource-id/bounds of the target widget). Optional key: 'delay_ms' (milliseconds to wait before tapping, default 100). Example: {'action':'quick_tap','feature':'<target_button_text>','delay_ms':100}. CRITICAL: Use 'quick_tap' instead of 'click' whenever the bug report describes a race condition, a tap that must happen immediately after a screen transition, or a button that only misbehaves when tapped quickly. Do NOT use 'quick_tap' for normal interactions — only use it when timing is the key factor in triggering the bug.

Understood. I will use 'quick_tap' for timing-sensitive bugs where a tap must occur immediately after a UI transition or within a short time window. I will use 'feature' to identify the target widget and optionally 'delay_ms' to control the tap timing. Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — NumberPicker / Spinner Scroll Action: 19.'picker_scroll': Advances an Android NumberPicker, TimePicker dial, or custom spinner widget by a whole number of ticks using a real scroll gesture. Use this when you need to change a value on a scrollable picker wheel (e.g. day / month / year pickers in date dialogs, hour / minute pickers in time dialogs, tuner-style NumberPicker widgets). Required key ONE of: (a) 'bounds' as a list [left,top,right,bottom] of the picker widget taken directly from the UI hierarchy, OR (b) 'x' and 'y' for the center coordinate of the picker. Required key 'to_direction': 'up' advances the value to the NEXT tick (e.g. May→June when direction='up' on month), 'down' goes to the PREVIOUS tick (May→April). Required key 'steps': how many ticks to advance (1 advances by one value). Example to advance a NumberPicker backward by 2 ticks with picker at bounds [X1,Y1,X2,Y2]: {'action':'picker_scroll','bounds':[X1,Y1,X2,Y2],'to_direction':'down','steps':2}. Use 'up' for the NEXT value in the wheel and 'down' for the PREVIOUS value. CRITICAL: Prefer 'picker_scroll' over 'set_text' or 'swipe_region' when the picker is a NumberPicker-style widget (you will see it as SCROLLABLE in the UI hierarchy with neighboring values like ['9','10','11'] or ['April','May','June']). 'set_text' on an inner EditText of a NumberPicker calls setValue() which validates the value and can bypass transient invalid-state windows some bugs need (e.g. holding a value on one picker while scrolling a related picker to a new value that would make the first value invalid). For bugs that crash during the SCROLL transition between values, 'picker_scroll' is the ONLY way to reproduce the crash. 'swipe_region' targets arbitrary screen coordinates and often overshoots or undershoots a single tick; 'picker_scroll' auto-computes the right tick distance from the picker bounds.

Understood. For NumberPicker / spinner-wheel widgets I will use 'picker_scroll' with either 'bounds' or 'x','y', a 'to_direction' of 'up' or 'down', and a 'steps' count indicating how many values to advance. I will prefer 'picker_scroll' over 'set_text' when the bug depends on the scroll transition holding a transient invalid state (e.g. holding an out-of-range value on one picker while scrolling a related picker). Please provide the app name, bug report, and the current app screen details to start the process.

IMPORTANT — Double-Tap Action: 20.'double_tap': Double-taps a target with standard double-tap timing. Use for normal double-tap gestures such as double-tap to zoom an image or map, double-tap to seek in a video player, or double-tap to select. Provide the target by 'x','y' screen coordinates (or the legend index of the widget, whose center is used). Example: {'action':'double_tap','x':540,'y':960}. This is a standard two-tap interaction; for timing-sensitive rapid taps that must bypass the inter-step settle, use 'quick_tap' instead.
Understood. I will use 'double_tap' for standard double-tap gestures and reserve 'quick_tap' for timing-sensitive rapid taps.
